originalTitle: 'Peace is a gradual thing': How land, cattle and identity fuel a deadly Nigerian conflict
title: Making Friends in Nigeria: How We Can Share and Be Kind!
content:
Hello, my little friends across Africa! It's your kid-friendly news reporter here, ready to tell you an important story from a beautiful country called Nigeria. We're going to talk about how everyone can be friends and share, even when things are a bit tricky.

Welcome to Our African News!

Hi everyone! I'm so happy to be with you today. We're going to learn about a place in Nigeria called Plateau State. It's a lovely place with pretty hills and friendly people. Just like your town or village, people in Plateau State want to live happily and safely with their families and friends. But sometimes, even in the nicest places, people can have disagreements. It's like when you and your friend both want to play with the same toy. Grown-ups can have bigger arguments, and sometimes, it can make people sad. We're going to talk about why these arguments happen and how we can all work towards being good friends.

Meet Our Friends in Plateau State

Plateau State is in the middle part of Nigeria. It's known for its cool weather and lovely scenery. Imagine green fields, big rocks, and lots of space. People live there, work there, and have their homes there. They love their homes very much, just like you love your home. Did you know? Nigeria is the country with the most people in all of Africa! It's a very big and important country with many different languages and cultures. In Plateau State, many people are farmers. This means they grow food like maize and beans. Other people are herders, which means they look after animals like cows. Both farming and herding are very important jobs that help feed everyone.

What's the Trouble About? Land!

One of the big reasons why people sometimes argue in Plateau State is because of land. Land is super important! It's where you build your house, where you play, and where farmers grow their food. For herders, land is where their cows can eat fresh grass. Imagine if two friends both wanted to build their sandcastles on the very same spot at the beach. That could be a problem, right? In Plateau State, sometimes farmers and herders disagree about who can use which piece of land. This can make people feel upset and even angry. Did you know? Land is also important for keeping our planet healthy, with trees and plants growing everywhere! When people can't agree on who owns or can use a piece of land, it can lead to bigger problems. It's like a small argument that grows and grows if no one helps to sort it out fairly.

Cattle! Our Hooved Friends!

Another big reason for disagreements is cattle. Cattle are cows, and they are very special animals in Africa! They give us yummy milk to drink and meat to eat. For many families, cows are also like their savings; they can sell them if they need money for school or food. Herders move their cattle to find good grass and water, especially when the weather is dry. Sometimes, these cows might walk onto a farmer's land and eat the crops that the farmer has worked so hard to grow. This is like your pet dog accidentally eating your sister's snack – it wasn't on purpose, but it still causes a problem! Did you know? A group of cows is called a herd! And baby cows are called calves! When cows eat a farmer's crops, the farmer loses their food and their money. This makes them very sad and angry. And the herder might say they didn't mean it, or that their cows needed food. It's a tricky situation where both sides feel hurt.

Being Different, Being Friends (Identity)

People in Plateau State, just like people everywhere, come from different families and groups. We call this their identity. Some people are mainly farmers, and others are mainly herders. They might have different traditions, speak different languages, or have different ways of dressing. It's wonderful to be different! Imagine if everyone wore the same clothes or liked the same food – how boring would that be? Being different makes the world colourful and exciting. But sometimes, people forget that even though we are different, we can still be friends and treat each other with kindness. Did you know? Nigeria has over 250 different ethnic groups, each with its own special traditions and stories! Sometimes, if people only think about their own group and don't try to understand other groups, it can make it harder to solve problems like sharing land or dealing with cows eating crops. They might think 'my group is right, and their group is wrong'. But true friendship means understanding both sides.

Why It's Hard to Be Friends: No Fairness (Justice)

When there are arguments, we need someone to help make things fair. We call this 'justice'. Imagine if you and your friend were arguing over a toy, and your teacher came to help. A good teacher would listen to both of you and find a fair way to share, so everyone feels happy. In Plateau State, sometimes people feel that when there's an argument about land or cows, no one helps to make things fair. Or, they might feel that the 'fair' decision always favours one group more than the other. This makes people feel very frustrated and sad. When people don't feel like they can get justice, they might try to solve the problem themselves. But sometimes, when people try to fix things on their own without fair help, the problems can get even bigger and more difficult to solve. It's like trying to fix a broken toy without asking for an adult's help – you might make it worse!

Why It's Hard to Be Friends: No Trust in Helpers (Security Forces)

Every community has special helpers whose job it is to keep everyone safe. These are like the police or soldiers, and we call them 'security forces'. Their job is to stop fights, protect people, and make sure everyone follows the rules. They are meant to be friends to everyone. But sometimes, people in Plateau State feel that they can't 'trust' these helpers. This means they don't believe that the police or soldiers will truly help them fairly, or that they will protect everyone equally. If you don't trust someone to help you, you might not go to them when you have a problem. Did you know? Police officers in many countries wear special uniforms to show they are there to help and protect people! If people don't trust the security forces, they might feel like they have to protect themselves and their families. This can lead to more fighting and sadness, because everyone is trying to take care of things on their own, instead of working together with trusted helpers.

What Happens When People Fight?

When these arguments grow too big, and people get very angry, sometimes there can be real fighting. This is very, very sad. Homes can be broken, people can get hurt, and families can lose their loved ones. Children might not be able to go to school, and everyone feels scared. Nobody wants fighting. Everyone wants peace. Peace means being safe, being happy, and being able to play and learn without fear. It means families can live together without worries, and neighbours can help each other.

How Can We Make Peace?

Making peace is like growing a beautiful flower. It takes time, care, and a lot of effort from everyone. Here are some simple ways to make peace: 1. **Talk and Listen:** When people have arguments, the first step is to talk to each other and, most importantly, listen to each other's feelings. Really hear what the other person is saying. 2. **Share and Understand:** Find ways to share the land and resources fairly. Understand that both farmers and herders need to live and work. 3. **Be Fair Helpers:** The police and soldiers need to show everyone that they are fair and helpful to all people, no matter which group they belong to. When helpers are fair, people will learn to trust them. 4. **Teach Kindness:** Grown-ups and children can all learn to be kind to everyone, even if they are different. Remember, we are all part of the big human family. Peace is a journey, not just a destination. It means working together every day, being patient, and always choosing kindness.

Your Part in Peace!

Even though you are small, you can help make peace! How? By being a good friend at school, by sharing your toys, by listening to your teachers, and by always being kind to everyone you meet. Every little act of kindness helps to build a more peaceful world. If we all try to understand each other, share fairly, and trust our helpers, then places like Plateau State in Nigeria can be happy and safe for everyone. Let's all wish for peace and work towards it, together!
summary: This news report for kids explains the challenges of conflict in Nigeria's Plateau State, caused by arguments over land, cattle, and identity, and highlights the importance of fairness and trust to build peace.
whyMatters: It matters because when people fight, everyone is sad and scared, but if we learn to share, be fair, and trust each other, everyone can live happily and safely together, just like good friends should.

originalTitle: Leading anti‑racism activist in Tunisia jailed for eight years
title: A Brave Helper in Tunisia
content:
A Brave Helper from Tunisia

Hello, my little friends! Welcome to our news from Africa. Today, we want to talk about someone very special. Her name is Saadia Mosbah. Saadia lives in a beautiful country called Tunisia. Tunisia is in North Africa. It has sunny beaches and lots of history. Saadia is a brave lady. She loves to help people. She is what we call an "activist." An activist is someone who works very hard. They work to make the world a better place. They help people who might be sad or in trouble. Saadia is one of these wonderful helpers. She wants everyone to be treated fairly. She wants everyone to be happy and safe. *Did you know?* Tunisia is famous for its ancient Roman ruins! Like big old buildings from a long, long time ago.

What is an Activist?

Let's talk more about what an activist does. Imagine you see your friend feeling sad. Maybe someone was not kind to them. You would want to help your friend, right? You might tell the teacher. You might give your friend a hug. An activist is like that, but for many more people. They speak up for people who cannot speak for themselves. They try to change things that are not fair. They might hold signs. They might talk to important people. They do all this because they believe in kindness. They believe in fairness for everyone. Saadia is an activist who believes in fairness for everyone. *Did you know?* Many good things in the world happened because activists worked hard to make them happen!

Being Kind to Everyone

Saadia is an "anti-racism" activist. This is a big word, but it means something simple and important. "Anti-racism" means saying "NO" to treating people badly. It means saying "NO" to being unkind to someone. Why would people treat others badly? Sometimes it's because of their skin color. Sometimes it's because of where they were born. Sometimes it's because of their religion. But "anti-racism" says everyone is equal. It says everyone deserves love and respect. No matter where they come from. No matter what they look like. Saadia believes this very strongly. She teaches others to be kind. She teaches everyone to be friends. She wants everyone to play together and share. She wants everyone to feel safe. *Did you know?* Africa is home to many different countries and cultures! Everyone in Africa is special and unique.

People Who Move: Migrants

Now, let's talk about some special people Saadia helps. She helps "migrants." What is a migrant? Imagine a little bird. This bird flies to a new place when the weather gets too cold. It moves to find a warmer, safer home. People can be like that too. Sometimes people have to leave their homes. Their homes might not be safe anymore. Maybe there is a big problem in their country. Maybe they don't have enough food. So, they move to a new country. They hope to find a safer place. They hope to find a new home. They hope to find food and schools for their children. These people are called migrants. They are looking for a new start. They are looking for a helping hand. *Did you know?* Many people around the world have moved to a new country at some point in their lives!

Saadia's Big Heart

Saadia has a very big heart. She saw these migrants coming to Tunisia. She saw that they needed help. Some of them were very sad. Some were scared. Some did not have a place to sleep. Saadia wanted to help them. She made sure they had food. She made sure they had a safe place to stay. She listened to their stories. She was a friend to them. She told everyone that these people were just like us. They deserved kindness. She campaigned for them. This means she worked hard to tell everyone about them. She wanted people to understand their struggles. She wanted people to welcome them. She wanted people to be good neighbors. One day, the President of Tunisia said something about migrants. The President is the leader of the country. He said that too many migrants would be a "threat." A threat means something that could cause a problem. He thought that having many people from other countries might be bad for Tunisia. But Saadia did not agree. She knew that these people needed help. She knew that kindness was more important. She kept helping them. She kept speaking up for them. She showed everyone that migrants are people too. They bring new ideas. They bring new songs. They bring new foods. They can make the country even better! *Did you know?* Sharing food and stories with new friends is a wonderful way to learn about the world!

The Sad News

This part of the story is a bit sad. Because Saadia helped migrants, something difficult happened. She was put in prison. When someone is put in prison, we say they are "jailed." This means she cannot go home for a long time. She was jailed for eight years. It's very sad because she was only trying to help people. She was trying to do good. She was trying to spread kindness. Many people are sad about this. They believe Saadia should be free. They believe she was doing the right thing. They believe that helping people should never lead to trouble. It is a time for us to think about what is fair. It is a time for us to think about what is right. We hope Saadia will be safe. We hope she will be well. We hope that one day she will be free. *Did you know?* In many countries, people believe that everyone should have the right to speak up for what is fair.

Why Helping is Important

This story makes us think. Why is it important to help others? When we help someone, we make them happy. We make the world a kinder place. Imagine if you fell down. You would want someone to help you up, right? That's what Saadia did. She saw people who needed help. She reached out her hand. Even when it was hard, she kept helping. She taught us that kindness is brave. She taught us that caring for others is very important. We must always try to be kind. We must always try to help. We must always remember that everyone deserves a friend. Everyone deserves a helping hand. Saadia showed us how to be a true friend. Saadia showed us how to be a true helper. *Did you know?* A single act of kindness can make a big difference in someone's day!

What Can We Do?

So, what can we learn from Saadia's story? We can learn to be kind to everyone. We can learn to welcome new friends. If someone new comes to your school, say hello! If someone looks sad, ask if they are okay. We can learn that everyone is special. Everyone deserves to be treated with respect. We can also learn to speak up. If you see something that is not fair, you can tell an adult. You can say that it's not right. Just like Saadia spoke up. We may be small, but we can all make a big difference. We can all spread kindness. We can all be like Saadia, a brave helper with a big heart. Let's make our world full of love and fairness, just like Saadia wants. *Did you know?* When you are kind to others, it makes you feel happy too!
summary: Saadia Mosbah, a kind helper in Tunisia who worked to welcome and assist people who moved from other countries, has been put in prison, which makes many people sad because she was trying to do good.
whyMatters: It is important because it makes us think about why we should always be kind to everyone, no matter where they come from, and how brave it is to help people even when it is hard.
